title: CFM Room Ventilation Calculator 2026 | Hacé Cuentas
description: Free CFM calculator for bathrooms, kitchens & rooms. Size exhaust fans and calculate ventilation needs based on air changes per hour. Instant results.
intro: Proper ventilation prevents moisture buildup, odors, and poor indoor air quality. Whether you're installing bathroom exhaust fans, kitchen ventilation hoods, or whole-home systems, calculating the correct CFM (cubic feet per minute) is essential. This calculator determines required ventilation based on room volume and air change rates. Calculate CFM ventilation for your space—accurate, free, and instant. Data is current as of 2026 and reviewed regularly.
keyTakeaway: Formula: **CFM = V × N/60**. Where V is room volume (cubic feet) and N is air changes per hour. Input your room dimensions and desired air change rate to get required CFM.
useCases: HVAC contractors sizing exhaust fans and ventilation systems
Homeowners planning bathroom or kitchen ventilation upgrades
Students and professionals studying ventilation engineering
Quick reference for ventilation codes and standards compliance

fields.label: Air Changes Per Hour
fields.help: Number of times the entire room volume is replaced per hour. Typical ranges: bathrooms 8, kitchens 10-15, living rooms 3-5, bedrooms 2-3.

explanation:
## Why Room Ventilation Matters

Proper ventilation removes moisture, odors, and airborne contaminants from your space. Building codes like ASHRAE 62 specify minimum ventilation rates to maintain indoor air quality and prevent mold, moisture damage, and health issues.

## How CFM Is Calculated

CFM stands for Cubic Feet Per Minute—the standard US measure of airflow. This calculator uses the fundamental formula:

**CFM = Room Volume (ft³) × Air Changes Per Hour ÷ 60**

The calculator converts your metric room dimensions to cubic feet, applies your desired air change rate, and outputs both CFM and metric equivalents (m³/h).

## Typical Ventilation Requirements by Room

- **Bathrooms**: 8 air changes/hour to manage humidity and moisture
- **Kitchens**: 10-15 changes/hour to exhaust cooking vapors and odors
- **Living rooms**: 3-5 changes/hour for general air quality
- **Bedrooms**: 2-3 changes/hour for fresh air circulation

## Final Notes

This calculation is a reference for initial system sizing. Local building codes, climate zone, humidity levels, and occupant count may require adjustments. For installation decisions, consult with an HVAC professional. Calculations are current as of 2026.

faq.q: What air change rates should I use for different rooms?
faq.a: Bathrooms typically need 8 air changes/hour. Kitchens require 10-15. Living rooms 3-5. Bedrooms 2-3. These rates are based on moisture generation and air quality standards.

faq.q: What types of exhaust fans can I use?
faq.a: Common options include wall-mounted fans (bathrooms), window units (temporary cooling/exhaust), ceiling fans (attics/bathrooms), kitchen range hoods (ducted or recirculating), and whole-home ventilation systems.

faq.q: How loud will my exhaust fan be?
faq.a: Noise levels under 30 dB are very quiet. 40-50 dB is acceptable. Above 60 dB becomes noticeable. Check fan specs for sone ratings—1 sone equals roughly 1 kW intensity.

faq.q: Do I need exhaust fans in every room?
faq.a: Bathrooms and kitchens require exhaust fans due to moisture generation. Other rooms benefit from ventilation depending on climate and use. Bedrooms need fresh air but can rely on passive infiltration in mild climates.
HRV systems capture heat or cooling energy from exhaust air before it leaves your home, recovering 70-90% of that energy. They're efficient in cold climates but add complexity and cost.

faq.q: Is this CFM calculator free to use?
faq.a: Yes. All Hacé Cuentas calculators are free without registration or login required.

faq.q: How accurate are these CFM calculations?
faq.a: These reference calculations use standard ASHRAE formulas validated internationally. They're reliable for initial system sizing. For critical installations, consult an HVAC engineer who can account for ductwork, filters, and installation specifics.

faq.q: Do you store my calculation data?
faq.a: No. All calculations run in your browser. We don't send your inputs to any server or store your data.
